claims.text: Perfectly logical safety measures often create a psychological counterforce, causing people to compensate by taking greater risks. For example, mandatory seatbelts in cars do not substantially improve road safety because drivers compensate by driving more dangerously, feeling secure in the seatbelt. Similarly, cyclists wearing helmets in London tend to have more accidents than those without, suggesting the sense of safety from the helmet causes behavior change that increases accident risk.
claims.speaker: unknown
conditions: safety measure is perceived as reducing personal risk
individual has discretion to adjust risk-taking behavior
claims.mechanism: Perceived safety reduces subjective risk awareness, causing people to increase risky behavior to restore their original risk tolerance level.
bounded_by: Does not apply to involuntary safety measures or passive protections
Cause may be cyclist behavior change or motorist behavior change; debate remains unresolved

claims.text: The seatbelt effect demonstrates that a logical intervention can create unintended behavioral consequences; drivers who wear seatbelts may drive slightly more dangerously because the seatbelt creates the subjective feeling of safety.
claims.speaker: unknown
conditions: driver has control over speed and driving aggressiveness
seatbelt adoption is voluntary or newly enforced
claims.mechanism: Subjective safety perception increases, causing driver to increase speed or aggression to restore felt-risk equilibrium.
bounded_by: The net safety effect of seatbelts is still positive if worn, despite behavioral compensation

claims.text: Cyclist helmet use in London correlates with higher accident rates, illustrating that a protective measure intended to reduce injury risk can be associated with increased accident frequency due to behavioral response.
claims.speaker: unknown
conditions: helmet adoption is observable and changes subjective risk
bounded_by: Causality unclear: may be cyclist behavior change or motorist behavior change in response to visible helmet
Helmet use is still recommended for injury reduction despite correlation with accidents

examples.text: Seatbelts: drivers who wear seatbelts feel safer and may drive more dangerously (e.g., higher speeds, more aggressive maneuvers), offsetting some of the safety benefit of the seatbelt itself.

Cyclist helmets in London: cyclists wearing helmets tend to have more accidents than those without, though it remains debated whether this is due to cyclists riding more aggressively after donning a helmet or motorists driving more aggressively around helmeted cyclists.
